claims.text: Malta hosts ~30 megalithic temples (Ggantija, Tarxien, Hagar Qim, Mnajdra) made of Limestone blocks weighing 20-50 tons stacked into multi-story walls with precise close-fitting joints - structures whose construction is hard to explain because Maltese Stone Age farmers had no metal tools and had not yet discovered the wheel.

claims.rationale: Malta's megalithic temples are real, internationally well-documented (UNESCO World Heritage), and the basic facts (50-ton limestone blocks, no contemporary evidence of metal tools or wheels in Malta during construction) are accurate. Mainstream archaeology accepts these as among the world's oldest free-standing stone structures. The 'how did Stone Age farmers build this' question is a real open question - mainstream answers (rolling logs, lever-and-fulcrum, communal labor over generations) are plausible but not definitively demonstrated.

claims.text: Maltese folk legend attributes the temples' construction to a single giantess named Sansuna who arrived on Gozo via a meteoric crash and belonged to the Nephilim, the Bible's hybrid offspring of fallen angels (the Watchers, per the non-canonical Book of Enoch) and human women, supposedly the target of God's Great Flood and (per Numbers) survivors who Moses's spies still encountered in Canaan.

claims.rationale: The Sansuna legend is a real Maltese folktale and Ggantija (Maltese 'gigantija') is named for it. The Book of Enoch's account of the Watchers and Nephilim is real (the text exists, is canonical for the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, was discovered in the Dead Sea Scrolls). The Numbers 13:33 'we seemed to ourselves like grasshoppers' verse about Nephilim survivors is real. So the host's reporting of these texts and legends is accurate. Whether the texts describe real events is a different question; treating them as physical history is what most religious-textual-historians don't do.

claims.text: In 1530 nobleman Giovanni (John) Abela began collecting alleged giant bones (femurs twice human size, oversized skulls, vertebrae, pelvic bones) from the Maltese temple ruins, in 1647 published a book documenting them, and turned his home into Malta's first museum to display them as evidence of a prehistoric race of giants.

claims.rationale: Gian Francesco Abela (1582-1655) was a real Maltese historian whose 'Della Descrittione di Malta' (1647) is real and is considered the first major work of Maltese historiography. He did record giant bones. The host's claim that he started in 1530 is a date error (Abela wasn't born then; the 1530 date belongs to the arrival of the Knights of St John on Malta - the host appears to have conflated dates). The bones-on-display claim is also documented in subsequent histories. The fundamental facts of Abela's giant-bone collection are accurate; the date detail is wrong.

claims.text: The Mnajdra temple's main and opposite axes align almost perfectly with the sun on the equinoxes and solstices today, but they would align *perfectly* in 10,200 BC - just after the end of the Younger Dryas - which is the only date when astronomical procession would make the alignment exact, suggesting the temple was originally laid out 12,000+ years ago, far older than mainstream dating allows.

claims.rationale: Mnajdra does have solar alignments on the equinoxes and solstices today; this is documented. The 'precession would make them perfect at 10,200 BC' argument is a Graham-Hancock-style claim that has been made by alternative-history authors but is contested by archaeoastronomers: Mnajdra's alignments are accurate to within a degree or two today, which is well within the construction-tolerance window without requiring a 12,000-year-old origin. Carbon dating of associated organic material in the Maltese temples consistently returns 3600-2500 BC, not 10,200 BC. The mainstream date stands; the host's interpretation requires rejecting carbon dating.

claims.text: The Hal Saflieni Hypogeum (rediscovered 1902) is a real subterranean three-level burial complex containing the remains of ~120 individuals (not the 7,000 sometimes cited), and includes 12 elongated skulls that have no sagittal suture - the seam between the parietal bones that should always be present in human anatomy - making them anatomically inhuman, although at least one shows signs of artificial cradleboard binding.

claims.rationale: The Hypogeum is real, was rediscovered in 1902, holds the remains of approximately 7,000 individuals (per Zammit's published reports the total is closer to 7,000 than 120 - the host's correction here may itself be wrong; archaeological literature commonly cites the higher number). The elongated-skulls claim is widely circulated, but craniologists who have re-examined the surviving skulls have concluded that the elongation is consistent with intentional cradleboard binding and that the 'no sagittal suture' claim is overstated - sutures in adult humans can become obliterated/closed (synostosis) and look invisible to casual inspection. The 'inhuman skull' interpretation is fringe; mainstream view is normal humans with cultural modification.

claims.text: The famous Lois Jessup story (Aug 1940 National Geographic) of seeing 20 25-foot-tall giants beckoning from a deep cavern in the Hypogeum is almost certainly fiction: the room types Jessup describes don't exist in the Hypogeum's lower levels, and the story is internally falsified by including the 'school class disappeared on a field trip' urban legend - a missing-children incident that, had it happened in the 20th century, would have been reported and isn't.

claims.rationale: The host's debunking is sound. Lois Jessup was a real woman who really did publish her giants-in-the-Hypogeum tale (the National Geographic 1940 attribution is contested - the story actually appears more reliably in Riley Crabb's 1962 'The Reality of the Cavern World'). Either way, the missing-school-children element is a verifiable folk legend not corroborated by any contemporary news report or coroner's record, and the layout Jessup describes doesn't match the actual Hypogeum. The host's conclusion 'probably just a story' is well-grounded.

dependencies.rationale: Specifies which parts of the giants-underground narrative are debunked, separating the genuine archaeological mystery from the fictional accretions.

claims.text: The host's verdict: most of the Maltese giants narrative is false (Abela's femurs were elephant bones, the Hypogeum's missing children is urban legend, Jessup's giants probably fiction), but the Mnajdra solar alignment threw him personally - if true, it would push the temples back 12,000+ years to the end of the Younger Dryas - and after years of hosting these stories he's moved from non-believer to 'maybe at one point very long ago great megalithic structures were built by a race of giants' who 'when the great flood came... boarded their chariots took to the sky and went back home.'

claims.rationale: The host's specific debunkings are correct (elephant bones for Abela's femurs is the real consensus answer; the missing children is folklore). His shift toward 'maybe giants did exist' is rhetorically honest - he's labeling it as a personal pivot, not making a knowledge claim. However the chariot-to-the-sky rescue is offered without any evidence and is essentially a religious/mythological pattern (the apotheosis trope). The verdict mixes good critical work on specific claims with credulous speculation on the broader thesis.

dependencies.rationale: Withdraws acceptance of Abela's giant-bone claim as the original framing supplied.
